title: Cirrus: timestamp all output script output

comments.body:
@edsantiago @rhatdan NNaaaooowww it's ready for PTAL :grin:  I'll save you some clicks, it looks like this:

```
[17:50:35] START
[+000001s] # Added by ./contrib/cirrus/setup_environment.sh, manual changes will be lost.
[+000001s] export EPOCH_TEST_COMMIT="fd39849beef3cdc6cce08814aaa1e12f60b37951"
[+000001s] export HEAD="fe625134b04ebff63b4db6a8fd8710cee46d2df7"
[+000001s] export TRAVIS="1"
...cut...
[+000069s] export GOGCCFLAGS="-fPIC="-m64 -pthread -fmessage-length=0 -fdebug-prefix-map=/tmp/go-build502634893=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches""
[+000069s] export PACKER_BUILDS="ubuntu-18,fedora-29,fedora-28,rhel-7"
[+000069s] 
[+000069s] ##### go version go1.11.5 linux/amd64 #####
[+000069s] 
[+000069s] TOTAL
[17:51:44] END
```

The TOTAL time even coincides with what Cirrus-CI reports (01:09) :smile: 

My only complaint is it does some oddball things when STDERR is involved, printing that out of sequence with STDOUT (if there is any).  Probably an easy way to fix that, but my brain is toast.
Ref: [What happens when there is only STDERR and no STDOUT](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5805093361287168)

I must emphatically continue insisting on HH:MM:SS. That is a format that every programmer in the world is used to and finds natural. This new format makes me think: is this 001s since the previous command? Since the start of the job? Is it how long the command took, or the previous one? Yes, I can figure it out by looking and comparing to the previous and next entries, but that will be a cognitive load -- not for you, because you'll be looking at the logs every day, but for everyone who looks at the logs infrequently. I believe in making things so intuitive that they do not need explanation or thinking.

As for stderr, just `|&`. For CI purposes I don't imagine there's a need to keep the streams separate; just please don't do this in a Makefile or any script intended to be run by humans, where it's useful to `>/dev/null` to see errors only.
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #2853) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.

> I must emphatically continue insisting on HH:MM:SS. 

There are two HMS's per command, top and bottom as a compromise, and because those absolute times are meaningful.  Otherwise, I simply disagree that ``+s`` offsets are hard to understand, in-context they fit nicely. Nearly everyone cares about performance (here) on the seconds-scale anyway.  So having minutes and hours is all but useless.  These logs are trivial to scrape for populating long-term statistics.  In big-data, the more calculation you can do up-front, the better off you are at ingest-time.

Lastly there's the merit argument:  If you insist loudly enough, it might be construed as your desire to help maintain the system :smile:  I'd take that compromise in a heartbeat.  I certainly don't see many other hands being raised to help out.   Heck, Matt and Giuseppe are probably the only other names you'd find across all the scripts.  Neither has complained about output formats, and yet found ways to make meaningful improvements without controversy.

> For CI purposes I don't imagine there's a need to keep the streams separate;

No, there's not a need for this, it's more useful to have the output serial.  Thanks for the tip.

This is interesting:

```
[+001647s] Ran 59 of 96 Specs in 218.309 seconds
[+001647s] SUCCESS! -- 59 Passed | 0 Failed | 0 Flaked | 0 Pending | 37 Skipped
```

It appears Ginkgo counts nowhere near the correct run-time :face_with_thermometer: .  See how the ``1647s`` clearly contrasts ``218 seconds``?  Directly comparable, no base-60 maths required, and a very stupid looking Ginkgo...it's loosing oodles and oodles of our seconds.  I want those back!
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #2865) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
LGTM other than @edsantiago 's comments.  Needs a rebase @cevich
@cevich Needs a rebase
LGTM once comments from @edsantiago are addressed

@edsantiago re:

> I do want to make triple-darn-sure that you've tested failure cases with pipes, and made completely certain that the |& will not mask a failure in any of the steps.

An excellent suggestion, and I too would like to be more sure on this.  Unf. our visibility into how commands are sent down to the VM and run by Cirrus-CI is a bit unclear/undocumented.  Based on what I've seen on the VMs, I believe each command, is fed down one-by-one, nested inside a shell script.  Then Cirrus executes the script.

That means we "should" be okay, since the ``contrib/cirrus/*.sh`` would be running a child-process of the top-level script (where the `|&` happens).  That top-level output is also what gets recorded into our logs bucket from every job.  Which (hopefully) means scraping it for data should be easy-ish.

If we need exact/detailed output, there's always using `tee` before the `|&' pipe, into a file.  Then cat the file (without the pipe).  For example: https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/2992

Anyway, yes, a closer examination of the output here is needed.

Update: I snuck onto a VM and looked.  Indeed, cirrus drops a script like this one in /tmp:

```
#!/bin/bash
set -e
set -o verbose
$SCRIPT_BASE/unit_test.sh
```

So after this PR, it would look like:

```
#!/bin/bash
set -e
set -o verbose
$SCRIPT_BASE/unit_test.sh |& ${TIMESTAMP}
```

Okay, here's example output after @edsantiago feedback:

(From gate task, build script)
```
/usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh podman |& ${TIMESTAMP}
[18:18:46] START - All [+xxxx] lines that follow are relative to right now.
[+0000s] touch .gopathok
[+0000s] go generate ./cmd/podman/varlink/...
[+0001s] go build -ldflags ' -X github.com/containers/libpod/libpod.gitCommit=d2c7474d86f0c8ad673681109397f0ab62139d46 -X github.com/containers/libpod/libpod.buildInfo=1556129927' -tags "seccomp   ostree selinux  varlink exclude_graphdriver_devicemapper" -o bin/podman github.com/containers/libpod/cmd/podman
[18:19:32] END - [+0046s] total duration since START
...cut...
```
@edsantiago @baude @TomSweeneyRedHat PTAL when u have time,  I think this one is ready to go.
@edsantiago ooof totally forgot, thx for reminding me...

@edsantiago okay, 9603962c5df6a9b2aaec860462d3098687e9cd9c should simulate a pretty deep failure.  That non-zero exit must propagate up through at least 3 process-layers, and many more in the case of rootless testing.
/hold

...hmmm...I s'pose there's also a scenario where the shell running the script pushed down from Cirrus fails.  That would be on the "wrong" side of the pipe.

However, while extremely unlikely, that parent process is the "agent" Cirrus installs to monitor the VM.  When that agent stops pinging back to their servers for any reason, Cirrus-CI assumes the VM is dead/broken and kills it.  I think if we ever get into that state, something is so extremely SNAFU, it's going to need hand-debugging anyway.

@edsantiago thx again for reminding me, it's easy to assume all green tests == nothing broken.

```
[+0020s] TESTING: THIS WILL EXIT 1
[+0020s] exit 1
[+0020s] make: *** [Makefile:191: ginkgo] Error 1
[19:28:04] END - [+0020s] total duration since START
```

So another approach is needed here, one that can set `-o pipefail` IIUC, ya?

I'm thinking instead of piping into awk, we need a wrapper script for every command, but that sucks.  Another idea is I could ask the Cirrus-CI devs how they feel about setting `pipefail` in their top-level script.  My hunch is they'd be open to the idea, and this could benefit others, so I'll start there...

@cevich thank you for testing. One possible approach might be to set `pipefail` in one of the YAML steps; another might be to do something like:
```
    - some-command |& timestamper
    + timestamper some-command
```
...that is, 'timestamper` is a simple script that sets pipefail, execs its arguments (careful with the quoting of course), and exits with the status of the command.
Okay, e-mail sent.  Let's see what they say.

@edsantiago I'm not certain setting in `yaml` would work,  I could be mistaken, but during initial development I'm pretty sure I found that Cirrus-CI sends each individual script string into it's own top-level script.  So it would literally 'set -o pipefail' and then exit :confounded:   I think the wrapper is the only option, so at this point a fix in Cirrus may be simpler (and benefit more than us).  I'm willing to wait and see what they say, they're normally pretty responsive (usually same-day service).
Opened RFE: https://github.com/cirruslabs/cirrus-ci-docs/issues/274
@edsantiago there we go, problem fixed for us, everyone else, and forever forth :+1:

Do I understand correctly that the Cirrus people just enabled `pipefail` by default? Does that take effect for just us, or for their entire infrastructure? If the latter, DAE feel just ever so slightly uncomfortable about this sort of change taking place just like that? Perhaps another customer was relying on pipefail being disabled, and will now be spending time trying to figure out why their builds are breaking; or perhaps we're relying on some behavior right now that they'll silently change in the future. Or have I misunderstood?

@edsantiago yep they sure did :D  (TBH I wasn't expecting it so fast either).  Yes some folks did/will have their jobs break. However, most likely:

0. Jobs that don't set `CIRRUS_SHELL: "/bin/bash"` are unaffected.  IIRC the default is `/bin/sh`.
1. Jobs that should have broken previously, weren't (incorrect assumptions / lack of bash knowledge).  Short-term surprise, long-term improvement (even if just learning about pipefail because of this).
2. Jobs that implicitly depended on an (arguably) bad default, now require explicit `+o pipefail`.  Their developer experience now has improved by readability / maintainability.
4. Jobs that use bash without pipefail support (FreeBSD apparently), [are now covered by improved integration tests on the Cirrus-side.](https://github.com/cirruslabs/cirrus-ci-docs/issues/275).

Keep in mind these guys are still a relatively new player, so some instability is expected.  At the same time, their support is top-notch and exceptionally fast to respond.  So for things like this it's worth asking.  The worst they can say is "no".  Conceivably there will be a point in time when Cirrus-CI is more heavily used, and more risk averse to usability improvements like this.  So if you have suggestions, please make them: RFE issue on the `cirrus-ci-docs` repo.
(rebase and --force push)
/hold cancel
Update: Added a small one-liner to actually confirm that pipefail is/remains set.
@edsantiago the vendor-failure is expected (master is broken also).   I added a quick `pipefail` validation check during gating in aef5bb4.  Good enough for now then?

/lgtm

This took a lot of detail work. Thanks for your close attention on it.
I'm going to hold /approve until the vendor check is fixed.
@cevich I think you need a rebase to fix the vendor check
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #2992) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
(rebased and `--force` pushed)
@mheon np, should be fixed now, and I'll rebase/`--force` push again just to be sure...
/approve

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *cevich*, *edsantiago*, *mheon* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Holding LGTM until tests green
Network flake, restarting test
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #3053) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
@cevich Needs another rebase :cry:
there we go.
/lgtm
/retest
/retest
Prow failing because of "disk pressure" - that seems like infra to me.
@stevekuznetsov Mind taking a look at this when you get a chance?

Yeah, we are hitting

https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1705657

You *should* be able to

/retest
Nice - thanks for looking!
